coredata.dc:title: The interaction between graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine and Au@Ag nanoparticles: Application as a fluorescence “turn-on” nanoprobe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Uddin, Imran
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine (GQDs-PEI) and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles blend was demonstrated to be a novel biosensing nanoprobe for the rapid and highly sensitive detection of biothiols such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcys) and glutathione (GSH). The fluorescence emission of GQDs-PEI was quenched efficiently upon interaction with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. The quenched fluorescence emission of the GQDs-PEI was restored in the presence of the biothiols. The fluorimetric sensing is based on the strong affinity between the mercapto (SH) groups of the biothiols and the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles by which the interaction between GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles was disrupted with a consequent modulation (‘turn-on’) of the quenched GQDs-PEI emission. Thus, a new, simple, rapid and highly sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe for detecting biothiols has been developed in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine
Gold and silver nanoparticles
Fluorescence ‘turn-on’

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs grafted with PEI (GQDs–PEI)
Optical properties and fluorescence quantum yields
Fluorescence quenching (turn-off) of GQDs-PEI by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles
Sensitivity of the GQDs-PEI/Au@Ag nanoprobe towards biothiols

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical illustration of the interaction between graphene quantum dots grafted with PEI (GQDs-PEI) and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles and the mechanism of biothiols sensing by the nanoprobe.
TEM images of (A) showing monodispersed grafted GQDs-PEI (B) Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles.
FT-IR spectra of (A) GQDs-PEI and (B) PEI.

Fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs grafted with PEI at different excitation wavelengths insert (a): Photographs of GQDs-PEI taken under visible light, pristine GQDs and GQDs-PEI under UV light (365nm). Insert (b) absorption spectrum of GQDs-PEI.
UV-vis absorption spectrum of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles.
High resolution XPS spectra of (A) Au and (B) Ag of the core-shell nanoparticles.

(A) Fluorescence emission quenching of GQDs-PEI in the presence of sequentially increased concentration of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. (B) Stern-Volmer plots showing the modified and unmodified quenching mechanisms. Solvent: 50mM PBS (pH 8.0). GQDs (0.5mg/mL), Au@Ag core-shell (0–200μg/mL). Excitation wavelength=350nm.

Fluorescence turn-on emission spectra of GQDs-PE-Au@Ag blend in the presence of Cys (A), Hcys (B) and GSH (C) in 50mM PBS solution (pH 8.0). GQDs-PEI concentration of 0.5mg/mL, Au@Ag (100μg/mL), respectively. Linear range: 0–90nM for Cys; 0–80nM for Hcys and 0–50nM for GSH, Excitation wavelength=350nm.

The selectivity (showing enhanced fluorescence intensity) of GQDs-PEI-Au@Ag core-shell nanoprobe for biothiols in the presence of other α-amino acids and proteins: GLY-Glysine, LYS-Lysine, HIST-Histidine, TRY-Trytophan, LAC-Lactic acid, Cys-Cysteine, Hcys-Homocysteine, GSH-Gluthatione, BSA-Bovine serum albumin, CYT C- Cytochrome c and LYZ-Lysozyme. Concentration for Cys, Hcys and GSH=50nM. Concentrations of α-amino acids=500nM, Concentrations of proteins=1.0mg/mL.
Optical and fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF), lifetimes (τ) of GQDs-PEI and pristine GQDs, quenching constants for GQDs-PEI and zeta potentials of GQDs-PEI and GQDs values also included.
Comparison of the sensitivity of our novel nanoprobe (GQDs-PEI-Au@Ag) with some reported analytical techniques for the detection of biothiols.

head.ce:title: The interaction between graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine and Au@Ag nanoparticles: Application as a fluorescence “turn-on” nanoprobe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence emission of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) grafted with polyethyleneimine (PEI) was “turned off” upon interaction with Au@Ag nanoparticles, and was “turned-on” in the presence of biothiols, resulting in a sensitive and selective fluorescence nanoprobe.

Graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine (GQDs-PEI) and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles blend was demonstrated to be a novel biosensing nanoprobe for the rapid and highly sensitive detection of biothiols such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcys) and glutathione (GSH). The fluorescence emission of GQDs-PEI was quenched efficiently upon interaction with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. The quenched fluorescence emission of the GQDs-PEI was restored in the presence of the biothiols. The fluorimetric sensing is based on the strong affinity between the mercapto (SH) groups of the biothiols and the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles by which the interaction between GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles was disrupted with a consequent modulation (‘turn-on’) of the quenched GQDs-PEI emission. Thus, a new, simple, rapid and highly sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe for detecting biothiols has been developed in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with excellent photoluminescence properties [1], hence have drawn a lot of attention for use as optical and fluorescence nanoprobes [2]. The photoluminescence properties of GQDs are determined by physical and chemical interactions taking place at their surfaces and edges. Such interactions may lead to either fluorescence quenching or enhancement [3–5].

The interactions of GQDs with biomolecules such as DNA, amino acids, cytochrome c, biothiols and melamine have been reported [6–10]. Also, GQDs have been grafted with polymers such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) to modify their surface and edge states in order to introduce desired functionality [11,12]. PEI is an organic polymer that has a high density of amino groups that can be protonated to give polycations [13]. The grafting of GQDs with PEI introduces positive charges on the surface of GQDs [11] and is an excellent surface passivation agent; hence it is employed in this work. However, to date the fabrication of a nanosensor based on GQDs grafted with polymers has not been reported. In this study, we report for the first time on the fabrication of a fluorescence “turn-on” nanoprobe composing of GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles for sensing. The fluorescence of the grafted GQDs is “turned off” by Au@Ag nanoparticles (NPs), and “turned on” by analytes. In this work we use biothiols as test analytes due to the presence of thiol groups. Strong metal-thiol (Ag-S and Au-S) bonds are expected to be formed between the biothiols and the Au@Ag nanoparticles.

Core-shell and alloy bimetallic nanoparticles are interesting because they provide opportunities to tune the optical properties of individual metal nanoparticles [14,15]. The bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles with combined properties of individual metallic elements are more interesting than single metallic nanoparticles [16].

The design and synthesis of nanoensembles or nanoprobes capable of binding and sensing biological molecules (biomolecules) selectively has attracted much attention in recent years because of the fundamental roles played by biomolecules in human systems and in chemical and environmental processes [17]. Among the biologically important molecules, biothiols are of particular interest due to the fact that biothiols (such as l-cysteine (Cys), dl-homocysteine (Hcys) and glutathione (GSH)) (which are used as examples in this work) are key players in cellular functions and take part in various redox reactions reversibly. The optical and fluorescence techniques developed so far for the detection of biothiols include; bovine serum albumin (BSA)-Ag nanoclusters, graphene oxide-Ru-DNA complex, GQDs-Hg2+ blend (not suitable for bio-applications due to Hg2+), G-quadruplex, carbon nanodots, semiconductors QDs (CdTe, CdSe/ZnSe) and metal nanoparticles (Au NPs) [18–25]. Most of these fluorimetric methods involve fluorescence ‘turn-off’ and/or cannot be used for biological applications due to toxicity issues. To date, the detection and quantitation of biothiols (Cys, Hcys and GSH) by fluorescence ‘turn-on’ (which is more reliable than “turn-off”) are few. The decrease in fluorescence (“turn off”) may not be only due to the effects of the test analytes, hence is less reliable. Fluorescence “turn on” designs for biomolecules (including thiols) detection have been widely explored in the literature [26–29], but not employing GQDs/Au@Ag system reported in this work.

Natural graphite powder (<20μm), branched polyethyleneimine (PEI), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9%), silver nitrate (AgNO3), l-cysteine, dl-homocysteine, glutathione (reduced), cytochrome c, histidine, lactic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, tyrosine, sodium borohydride, Rhodamine 6G and dialysis membrane tubing (MWCO 1.5kDa) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Tryptophan was obtained from Fluka, glycine was purchased from SAAR Chem. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without prior purification. All solutions including phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were prepared with ultra-pure water obtained from a Milli-Q Water system (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). Other reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received.

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. The excitation wavelengths and slit width (each 5nm) were kept constant for all the experiments. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range 300–800nm. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum ATR. A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (l=1.5405A°, nickel filter). Data were collected in the range 2θ=10°–60°, scanning at 1°min−1 with a filter time-constant of 2.5s per step and a slit width of 6.0mm. Details have been given previously [30]. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 200, Picoquant GmbH) as previously described [30].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer, the following parameters were used: the emission was 10mA, the anode (HT) was 15kV and the operating pressure below 5×10−9 Torr. A hybrid lens was used and resolution to acquire scans was at 160eV pass energy in slot mode. The center used for the scans was at 520eV (width of 1205eV) with steps at 1eV and dwell time at 100ms as reported before [31]. High resolution scans were acquired using 80eV pass energy in slot mode. The chemically distinct species were resolved using a nonlinear least squares curve fitting procedure. The core level binding energies (BEs) were aligned with respect to the C1s binding energy (BE) of 285eV.

A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data. Sample preparation and procedures have been previously reported [32]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80kV. Dynamic light scattering experiments and Zeta potential measurements were done on a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out using a Berghof (Germany) High Pressure Laboratory Reactor (highpreactor) BR-300, V.3.0 equipped with PT-100 temperature and pressure sensors, BTC-300 Temperature regulator and manometer and PTFE lining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs grafted with PEI (GQDs–PEI)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) from natural graphite powder was carried out by Hummers method as reported previously [33]. GQDs-PEI was synthesized by the top-down hydrothermal method adopted from the literature with some modifications [11]. Briefly: GO (0.5g) was oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (30mL) for 4h under ultrasonication. The mixture was then diluted with Millipore water (100mL) and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane. The pH of the oxidized GO was adjusted to 8.0 using 10% NaOH. Aqueous solution of PEI (10mL of 2.5mg/mL) was added to the obtained GO suspension in a glass beaker, followed by vigorous stirring for 2h after which the mixture was transferred to a 400mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated up to 160°C for 8h. The product was left to cool to room temperature and filtered using a 0.22μm membrane. The collected colloidal solution was further dialyzed for two days using a dialysis membrane (MW 1.5kDa) to remove unreacted PEI, acids and salts, giving GQDs-PEI. We also prepared GQDs without PEI following similar synthetic route and conditions for comparison.

Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were synthesized following a procedure previously described with some modification [34]. Briefly: 0.01g of NaBH4 was added to a 100mL solution of 10−4 M HAuCl4 in Milli-Q water followed by vigorous stirring. This reaction is accompanied by the immediate appearance of deep ruby red colour characteristic of gold nanoparticles. This solution was allowed to stand for a period of 24h before further use. Prior to coating with tyrosine, the gold nanoparticles solution was heated in order to remove the excess sodium borohydride. The as-synthesized gold nanoparticles were capped with tyrosine by addition of 10mL of an aqueous solution of 10−3 M tyrosine to 90mL of the gold nanoparticles solution. This solution was also allowed to stand for a period of 24h before further use. For the synthesis of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles, 90mL tyrosine-capped gold nanoparticles solution was mixed with 10mL of 10−3 M AgNO3 and 1mL of 10−1 M KOH solution. Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were formed following the reduction of Ag+ ion by the tyrosine capping the Au nanoparticles (which is pH dependent and occurs in alkaline medium, hence the use of KOH). The solution was boiled until its colour changed to brownish yellow. The Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were thus obtained.

Fluorescence quantum yields of the GQDs-PEI and GQDs (without PEI) were determined by the comparative method [35], Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ F
=
Φ F
S t d
F
A
S t d
n
2
F
S t d
A
(
n
S t d
)
2
where A and A Std are the absorbances of the sample and the standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. F and F Std are the areas under the fluorescence curves of the GQDs-PEI and the standard, respectively and n and n Std are the refractive indices of the solvent used for the sample and standard, respectively. Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (ΦF Std =0.94 [36]) was used as the standard.

Colloidal aqueous solutions of PEI functionalized GQDs of different concentrations (500μL of 0.5mg/mL, 1.0mg/mL and 2mg/mL) were titrated with various concentration of Au@Ag core-shell colloidal solution (0–200μg/mL) in 50mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (2mL, pH 8.0). This pH was employed since GQDs are known to be weakly fluorescent in acidic medium [37]. The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs-PEI were recorded under excitation wavelength of 350nm. The working solutions were vigorously stirred (at room temperature) prior to photoluminescence measurements. The synthesized pristine GQDs (without PEI) were also titrated with similar concentrations of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles so as to compare the fluorescence properties of the PEI grafted GQDs with the pristine GQDs in the presence of Au@Ag core-shell system.

Aqueous solutions of different concentrations of Cys, Hcys and GSH were freshly prepared before use. A typical detection procedure was conducted (after optimization) as follows: 500μL (0.5mg/mL) GQDs-PEI was mixed with 1000μL (100μg/mL) of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles colloidal solution followed by the addition of 2mL of 50mM PBS of pH 8.0. Different concentrations of the biothiols prepared via serial dilutions (0.01M biothiols stock solutions were first prepared) were incubated in vials containing the GQDs-PEI/Au@Ag mixture and equilibrated for 2min at room temperature. The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-PEI were recorded (before and after the addition of different concentrations of biothiols) in the wavelength range of 360–600nm with an excitation wavelength of 350nm.To further explore the selectivity of this method, the abilities of other five α-amino acids (histidine, lactic acid, tyrosine, glycine and tryptophan) and three proteins (cytochrome c, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and lysozyme) to interfere in the recovery (turn-on) of the quenched fluorescence emissions of the GQDs-PEI in the presence of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were also investigated. Both the α-amino acids and proteins were at 10-fold higher concentrations than Cys, Hcys and GSH.

TEM image (Fig. 1 A) of GQDs-PEI shows the morphology and size distribution of the GQDs grafted with PEI. The GQDs-PEI are monodispersed with an overall quasi-spherical morphology with particles size ranging from 7 to 15nm, with an average of 10.5nm which is in close agreement with the average size obtained from DLS experiment (Fig. S1A). It could be observed from the TEM image that the GQDs-PEI were not aggregated, which is ascribed to PEI as a surface passivation agent [38]. The particle size of the pristine GQDs was in the range 2–6nm, hence smaller than GQDs-PEI.
The XRD pattern (Fig. 2 ) of the GQDs-PEI showed a broad peak centered at 2ϴ=25.6° which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphene based nanomaterials [39].

Raman spectrum of the GQDs-PEI (Fig. 3 ) shows the distinctive features typical of sp2 and sp3 of C in carbon-based nanostructures. The disordered (D) band related to the presence of sp3 defects and the G band related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbons were observed at 1458 and 1591cm−1, respectively. The shifting of the D and G bands (compared to that of pristine GQDs occurring ca at 1384 and 1553cm−1, respectively) (Fig. S2) might be due to the grafting with PEI. Surface passivation/doping have been described to lead to defects in the lattices and stretching of the graphene layers [40,41]. The ratios of the intensities (ID/IG) of D and G bands have been used to correlate the structural properties of carbon nanomaterials. The ID/IG ratios for pristine GQDs and GQDs-PEI were found to be 0.51 and 0.85, respectively. Thus, the defects increased with the grafting of the GQDs.

FT-IR spectra of GQDs-PEI and PEI alone are shown in Fig. 4 . The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-PEI (Fig. 4A) show vibrations that are present in PEI alone (Fig. 4B) (NH at 3269 and 1584cm−1, CH2 at 2930 and 2805cm−1, CN at 1112cm−1). The broad peak of the GQDs-PEI spectrum between 3545 and 3126cm−1 could be due to the existence of NH and OH groups on the grafted GQDs. The vibration around 1642cm−1 in the IR spectrum of GQDs-PEI could be associated with CO of amide and carboxylic groups of the GQDs-PEI and further indicates that grafting of the GQDs in situ was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence quantum yields
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs-PEI and GQDs show a light yellow colour under visible light (Fig. 5, insert (a)), but exhibited bright blue fluorescence (under 365nm UV) coupled with maximum fluorescence intensity at 448nm (for GQDs-PEI) at an excitation wavelength of 350nm, Table 1.

The fluorescence emissions of GQDs-PEI were recorded at different excitation wavelengths (300–370nm); the emissions were excitation-independent with no noticeable shifts in emission peaks recorded at the respective excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5). Excitation-independent properties are not uncommon in GQDs [32,42]. Zeta potential measurements show that pristine GQDs (without PEI) are negatively charged (Fig. S1B), Table 1. However, upon grafting the GQDs with PEI, positively charged GQDs-PEI were obtained (Fig. S1C) due to the fact that PEI is polycationic with a high density of amino groups in its branched structure, this result is the same as reported in the literature [11]. Thus, the successful grafting of the GQDs by PEI is further confirmed by this result.

As previously reported, the use of tyrosine to cap the Au nanoparticles is helpful for the reduction of silver ions into its nanoparticles form and results in the formation of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles [43]. Au@AgNPs show a peak at 489nm (Fig. 6 ) which corresponds to excitonic transition in the core-shell nanocrystallites [44].

The TEM image of Au@Ag nanoparticles depicts mainly quasi-spherical morphology (Fig. 1B). The average particle size of the Au@Ag core shell nanoparticles were determined to be around 15nm. These nanoparticles show contrast features with a dark centre and a lighter shell, which reveals the core-shell architecture.

The chemical composition of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles was analyzed using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). All signals were charge corrected taking the adventitious carbon C 1s core level signal observed at 285eV as reference, the chemically distinct species were resolved using a nonlinear least squares curve fitting procedure. The photoelectron emission showed the Au 4f orbital of gold nanoparticles, which indicates the presence of gold (Fig. 7 A). The Au 4f core level signal could be resolved into Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 at binding energies of 84.09 and 87.74eV, respectively. These are assigned to metallic (Au°) states of gold nanoparticles. Fig. 7B shows Ag 3d spectrum which could be resolved into two peaks (3d5/2 and 3d3/2) due to the spin-orbit coupling with binding energies of 368.21 and 374.17eV, respectively. These binding energy components are attributed to metallic silver in the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching (turn-off) of GQDs-PEI by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: It has been widely reported that fluorescence quenching can occur between unbonded fluorophores and quencher molecules (upon simple mixing) [21,46]. The simple mixing has been described as important due to the fact that some properties which are key to bio-assays and bio-applications are preserved [47]. In this study, the interaction of GQDs-PEI with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles was electrostatic in nature upon simple mixing since the GQDs grafted with PEI (a polycationic polymer) possess positive surface charges, while Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles are negatively charged. The tyrosine capping of Au nanoparticles helped in the reduction of the Ag ions to form the core-shell structure under alkaline condition, resulting in the negative charge for Au@Ag. Surface negative charges of the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were further confirmed by zeta potential measurement (figure not shown).

From preliminary runs, it was discovered that the GQDs-PEI fluorescence emission was efficiently and sequentially quenched in the mixture with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles in buffered aqueous solution. However, this was not the case for the pristine GQDs (without PEI), as the fluorescence emission was poorly quenched by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles and was ascribed to the weak or non-interaction between the pristine GQDs and the Au@Ag core-shell system since they both possess similar surface charges (both negatively charged surface). Due to this observation, further studies were carried out using GQDs-PEI only, having established its superior characteristics to function as a better nanoprobe compared to the pristine GQDs for this particular study.

The emission spectra of the GQDs-PEI were recorded upon the titration of a fixed concentration of the GQDs-PEI (500μL, 0.5mg/L) with varying concentrations of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased (turned off) as the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles concentration was sequentially increased. The fluorescence “turn off” is represented in Scheme 1 .

Stern-Volmer relationship was used to assess the nature of the quenching mechanism and a non-linear trend was observed with a positive deviation from linearity (Fig. 8B). Deviations of this kind could mean that both static and dynamic quenching occurred simultaneously [47].

A modified Stern-Volmer Eq. (2) was used to determine the dynamic and static quenching constants following a method previously described [48]
(2)
F 0
F
. e x p
K
[
A u @ A g
]
= 1 +
K
S V
[
A u @ A g
]
where F0 and F are fluorescence emission intensity in the absence and presence of the quencher (Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles, in this case), respectively; [Au@Ag] represents the concentration of the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles as the quencher. K and KSV are the static and dynamic quenching constants, respectively. To determine the static and dynamic quenching constants, the modified Stern-Volmer Eq. (2) was used to fit the upwardly curved plots into linear plots (Fig. 8B) after which K and KSV were thus evaluated (Table 1). The values of K and KSV are 2.5×104 and 1.6×103, respectively.

The quenching of GQDs-PEI emissions by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles via static and dynamic mechanisms as observed in this study is ascribed to both collisional (diffusion controlled) and electrostatic (static) interactions between GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles have negative charges on their surface as stated above, whereas the GQDs-PEI possess a positively charged surface (due to the polycationic tendencies of PEI), hence electrostatic interactions is a plausible reason for the observed static fluorescence quenching of GQDs-PEI by Au@Ag core-shell system. Dynamic quenching would normally occur due to collisional interaction between fluorophores and quenchers in a diffusion controlled surface absorption or interaction. As shown above and in Table 1, the value of K representing the static quenching constant is larger than KSV which represents the dynamic quenching component of the GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag interaction. It can be concluded that static quenching dominates hence a larger value (Table 1). This is also in agreement with the fact that electrostatic interaction would largely result in static quenching and since both nanomaterials of GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag possess oppositely charged surfaces, static quenching is more favoured. Similar observations have been reported in fluorescence quenching involving both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms where the interaction between fluorophores and quenchers would have larger values of static quenching constants (K) in systems involving electrostatic interaction with the possibility of surface diffusion or collision also leading to dynamic quenching [48,49].

Prior to the ‘turn-on’ experiments using the nanoprobe, a couple of factors which might affect the interaction of the GQDs-PEI-Au@Ag blend with the biothiols were optimized. We optimized the GQDs-PEI concentration suitable for the biothiols detection. We studied the concentration of GQDs-PEI (0.5, 1.0, 2.0mg/L) in the presence of 100μg/mL Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles and established that 0.5mg/mL is most suitable for interaction with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles since the GQDs-PEI emissions were most efficiently quenched at this concentration. From the quenching experiments, it was observed that at Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles concentration higher than 100μg/mL, there was a positive deviation from linearity in the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 8B). The best signal was also observed at 100μg/mL. Owing to these observations, 100μg/mL of Au@Ag NPs was employed.

The pH of the aqueous phosphate buffer solution (PBS) employed was also optimized. GQDs are known to have weak or no fluorescence activity in acidic media [37]. Mercapto group (SH) of biothiols has also been reported to get oxidized at pH>9.0 to generate the disulphide bond [19], hence we tested the biothiols detection in the pH range 7–10. As shown in Fig. S3, there were no significant differences in recovered fluorescence emission (F/F0) at pH 7.0 and 9.0. Hence, pH 8.0 was selected for the detection procedures. Incubation time was also assessed; the recovered fluorescence (F/F0) (figure not shown) increased rapidly within 2min and remained constant over time. This implies that this ‘turn-on’ procedure for biothiols quantitation could be carried out in a very short time (within 2min) which is also an added advantage of this nanoprobe. The analysis time is among the shortest for biothiols detection reported thus far, Table 2 .

Results obtained from our study under optimized conditions show rapid and increasing recovery of the fluorescence emission of the GQDs-PEI upon the addition of increasing concentration of Cys, Hcys and GSH, respectively (Fig. 9 A, B and C), as a result of fluorescence “turn on” shown in Scheme 1. We theorize that the addition of the respective biothiols to the GQDs-PEI-Au@Ag disrupted the interactive relationship between GQDs-PEI and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. This is ascribed to the known strong affinity of Au and Ag for thiol groups and their tendencies to preferentially bind with the −SH groups of biothiols to form the very strong AgS and/or AuS bonds [50,51]. Binding of biothiols (via −SH group) on the surfaces of the Au and Ag nanoparticles through covalent bonding have been reported [52,53]. Thus, GQDs-PEI was released from its electrostatic interaction and or surface adsorption with Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles due to the stronger binding interaction between the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles and the respective biothiols (Cys, Hcys and GSH). In addition to the recovered fluorescence emission, there were no changes in the emission wavelength or distortion of the emission spectra, which points to the fact that the restored emission originates from the GQDs-PEI rather than any likely new products. Also, the addition of the respective biothiols to the colloidal solution of the GQDs-PEI (in the absence of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles) show no effect on the emission intensity of the GQDs-PEI under similar experimental conditions which prove that the restored fluorescence was significantly due to the preferential binding of the Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles to the biothiols with a consequent recovery of the quenched fluorescence emission of the GQDs-PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the GQDs-PEI/Au@Ag nanoprobe towards biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: In order to establish the sensitive nature of the probe, the limits of detection (LODs) for the biothiols were evaluated from the plots shown in Fig. 9 (insets) and using 3δ/K (where δ is the standard deviation of blank measurement (n=12) and K is the slope of the calibration graphs). The [(F-F0)/F0] values were plotted against the biothiols (Cys, Hcys and GSH) concentrations and the linear ranges were obtained to be 0–90nM for Cys; 0–80nM for Hcys and 0–50nM for GSH, respectively (Fig. 9 insets and Fig. S4). Interestingly, the LODs were as low as 1.5nM, 3.0nM and 4.5nM for Cys, Hcys and GSH, respectively (Table 2). It follows from the obtained detection limits that this assay is highly sensitive with the lowest detection limits compared to biothiols sensing nanoprobes reported to date (Table 2). GQDs based sensors for biothiols so far reported are just two, one involves a ‘turn-off’ method (which are mostly not reliable) and the other a ‘turn-on’ approach, but with Hg2+ as part of the probe which limits its biological application due to toxicity concerns [9,21]. The results show that the detection sensitivity is in the order Cys>Hcys>GSH. GSH showed the least sensitively among the biothiols and this has been attributed to steric hindrance [9,19,21,23]. We presume that due to the bulky nature of GSH (compared to Cys and Hcys) and the presence of more functional groups, the effective binding to Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles is sterically restrained. We judge from the detection limits of our nanoprobe for biothiols sensing, that its application can be achieved in biological media coupled with a rapid detection time. Other interesting features of this nanoprobe as compared to other organic and semiconductor QDs fluorophores reported to date for biothiols sensing include: fluorescence ‘turn-on’ (which is more reliable than the widely reported turn-off methods), non-toxicity of GQDs [54], excellent water dispersibility, high photostability, high photoluminescence properties at physiological pH and ease of functionalization and doping. For example, the GQDs are grafted with a functional moiety such as PEI which is employed as delivery agent and attachment factor in cell studies [13].

To test the selectivity of the probe with respect to biothiols sensing without interferences from other molecules, the relative fluorescence emission intensity of the recovered emission was assessed in the presence of five common α-amino acids (glysine, lysine, histidine, tryptophan and dl-lactic acid) and three proteins (bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, and cytochrome c) under similarly optimized conditions (Fig. 10 ). It was interesting to observe remarkable differences in the relative fluorescence intensities between that recorded for the respective biothiols and other α-amino acids and proteins. The difference is the presence of SH in Cys, Hcys and GSH. Au and Ag have a stronger affinity for SH than NH or NH 2 groups present in the other α-amino acids and proteins. This further adds to the analytical figures of merits of this reported method for biothiols detection with a high degree of selectivity.

Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were employed to quench the fluorescence emission of the cationic polymer grafted GQDs. However, the fluorescence emission of GQDs-PEI in the GQDs-PEI@Au@Ag core-shell blend was restored reversibly in the presence of biothiols as test analytes. Based on this phenomenon, and the modulation of the restored GQDs-PEI emission as a function of the respective biothiols concentrations, we have developed a new method for biothiols detection. This method offers the highest sensitivity for biothiols detection reported so far involving GQDs-based biosensing designs and highly selective towards the detection of glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), and homocysteine (Hcys) in the presence of other α-amino acids and proteins. In addition, this probe can be deployed in bio-applications for sensing biothiols with rapidity, simplicity and specific response to biothiols as added advantages over existing methods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2016.03.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
